data.title: AM Markets Need to Know: Meta and AI, Trump's naval blockade, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures were muted as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note.

Now, here are 5 news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

Meta shifts spending toward AI, signals more layoffs possible: Meta (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the company’s planned job cuts stem from increased capital spending for artificial intelligence and did not rule out further layoffs, Reuters reported. “We basically have two major cost centers in the company: compute infrastructure and people-oriented things,” he said during a town hall on Thursday. “If we’re investing more in one area to serve our community, then that means we have less capital to allocate to the other.”

Blue Owl crystalizes tenfold return on SpaceX stake: Blue Owl Capital (OWL) co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said the company has sold about half of its stake in SpaceX (SPACE) at a $1.25T valuation, realizing roughly a tenfold return. “We made about 10x our money,” Lipschultz said on an earnings call, noting the firm still holds about half of its position. Blue Owl was among SpaceX’s earliest lenders and later converted its exposure into an equity investment.

Trump maintains Iran naval blockade amid oil price surge: The U.S. and Iran continue to lock horns in a prolonged standoff, with President Donald Trump reaffirming his commitment to a naval blockade of Iranian ports despite surging oil prices and global economic concerns. Trump said the blockade is effectively “choking” Iran’s economy and signaled it would remain in place until Tehran agrees to curb its nuclear program. “Their economy is crashing; the blockade is incredible,” Trump told reporters at the White House.

Trump signs executive order to expand retirement savings access: President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Thursday to create a government website allowing people in the U.S. to find and compare private-sector retirement savings plans. The aim is to help more Americans sign on for retirement plans before next year, when the federal government will start matching retirement contributions made by low- and middle-income workers.

Trump grants permit reviving portions of Keystone XL pipeline: President Trump signed an order Thursday granting a cross-border permit to the Bridger pipeline expansion project that would revive parts of the Keystone XL pipeline to transport Canadian oil to Guernsey, Wyoming. The pipeline, proposed by South Bow (SOBO) and U.S. partner Bridger Pipeline, would travel 647 miles through eastern Montana and Wyoming, carrying 550K bbl/day of Canadian crude and potentially increasing Canada’s exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.

data.title: Chevron Non-GAAP EPS of $1.41 beats by $0.44, revenue of $48.61B misses by $4.09B
data.description:
• Chevron press release (CVX): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.41 beats by $0.44.
  • Revenue of $48.61B (+2.1% Y/Y) misses by $4.09B.
  • Worldwide and U.S. production increased by 15 and 24 percent, respectively
  • The company returned $6.0 billion of cash to shareholders during the quarter, including share repurchases of $2.5 billion and dividends of $3.5 billion.
  • The company’s Board of Directors declared a quarterly dividend of one dollar and seventy-eight cents ($1.78) per share, payable June 10, 2026, to all holders of common stock as shown on the transfer records of the corporation at the close of business on May 19, 2026.
  •

data.title: Proto Labs Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 beats by $0.15, revenue of $139.3M beats by $4.16M
data.description:
• Proto Labs press release (PRLB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 beats by $0.15.
  • Revenue of $139.3M (+10.4% Y/Y) beats by $4.16M.
  • CNC machining revenue grew 19.7% year-over-year.
  • Revenue per customer contact increased 20.4% year-over-year.

  • Financial Guidance and Outlook:

    • In fiscal year 2026, Protolabs expects to generate revenue growth between 6% and 8%.
    • In the second quarter of 2026, the Company expects to generate revenue between $140.0 million and $148.0 million.
    • In the second quarter of 2026, the Company expects diluted net income per share between $0.29 and $0.37, and non-GAAP diluted net income per share between $0.50 and $0.58. 
  • Shares +5% PM.

data.title: Apple CEO flags rising memory costs as ongoing headwind
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook flagged rising memory prices, which are expected to weigh more heavily on the company’s costs in coming quarters.

Speaking on the company’s earnings call on Thursday, Cook said memory costs are set to be "significantly higher" in the June quarter and will have a larger effect beyond that.

Memory costs had a "minimal impact" in the December quarter but rose in the March period, partly offset by carry-in inventory benefits, Apple said. 

"Beyond the June quarter, we believe memory costs will drive an increasing impact on our business," Cook said.

"We'll continue to evaluate this, we'll look at a range of options"

Apple on Thursday reported strong second-quarter earnings driven by strong demand for the iPhone 17. Per-share profit was $2.01, beating estimates by $0.07, on revenue of $111.18B, which grew 16.5%, also ahead of expectations.

Q1 gross margin of 49.3% did include the impact of tariff-related costs. Cook also said tariffs had a smaller impact in the March quarter compared with the prior period due to lower product volumes and reduced rates, adding the company would reinvest any tariff refunds into U.S. innovation and manufacturing.Shares are up 3.2% in premarket trading on Friday.

data.title: Hudbay Minerals Non-GAAP EPS of $0.40 beats by $0.06, revenue of $757.3M beats by $70.14M
data.description:
• Hudbay Minerals press release (HBM): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.40 beats by $0.06.
  • Revenue of $757.3M (+27.3% Y/Y) beats by $70.14M.
  • Reaffirmed full year 2026 consolidated production guidance including 110,000 to 138,000 tonnes of copper and 217,000 to 272,000 ounces of gold. Reaffirmed 2026 cost guidance, including consolidated cash costi guidance of $(0.30) to $(0.10) per pound of copper and sustaining cash costi guidance of $1.70 to $2.10 per pound of copper.

data.title: Bio-Rad outlines 2026 currency-neutral revenue growth of -3% to +0.5% amid Middle East disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jonathan DiVincenzo (President & COO) said Bio-Rad “reported Q1 results within our revenue guidance” while facing “several external pressures, most notably associated with the ongoing conflict in the Middle East,” adding that in 2025 the region “represented over 9% of our Diagnostics segment, primarily driven by our blood typing franchise,” and that the conflict “will be a significant headwind for revenue and margin for full year 2026.”

  • DiVincenzo said Life Science “reported net sales were flat,” with “academic demand…constrained, particularly in the Americas,” and noted Bio-Rad’s customer checks suggest “there continues to be considerable disruption,” plus “a lag between funding approvals and purchasing activity.” He added, “In biopharma, we are seeing early signs of stabilization,” with “later-stage companies…more robust,” and Bio-Rad expects “gradual improvement through the year.”

  • DiVincenzo highlighted digital PCR momentum: “ddPCR instrument revenue grew 24% over prior year,” and said the QX700 platform is “driving both competitive wins and conversion from qPCR,” with “over 99% of our digital PCR assays…available on the new QX700 series.”

  • Roop Lakkaraju (Executive VP & CFO) reported “net sales…approximately $592 million,” and said Bio-Rad’s non-GAAP results were “$51 million or $1.89 diluted earnings per share,” while the Sartorius fair-value change “contributed $562 million to our reported net loss of $527 million or $19.55 per diluted share.”

  • Norman Schwartz (Chairman & CEO) said Bio-Rad’s M&A posture has evolved: “our focus has shifted from early-stage opportunities to companies with demonstrated revenue and margin profiles,” targeting “companies within the $100 million to $500 million revenue range,” adding, “We’re not, at the moment, focused on anything transformative.”

Outlook

  • Lakkaraju said, “We have decided to adjust our 2026 guidance,” citing Middle East demand softness and logistics, and guided full-year currency-neutral revenue growth “between minus 3% and plus 0.5%,” with Life Science currency-neutral growth “between minus 3% and minus 1%” and Diagnostics “between minus 3% and plus 1%.”

  • Lakkaraju projected “full year non-GAAP gross margin…between 53% and 54%” and “full year non-GAAP operating margin…between 10% and 12%,” and said Bio-Rad updated full-year free cash flow to “approximately $290 million to $340 million.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance, Bio-Rad moved from currency-neutral revenue growth of “between 0.5% and 1.5%” and non-GAAP operating margin of “between 12% and 12.5%” to the updated ranges Lakkaraju provided in Q1.

Financial Results

  • No reliable analyst estimates comparison was included because the provided estimates data does not use the transcript-required quarter format (Q1–Q4).

  • Lakkaraju said Life Science segment sales were “$229 million,” and Diagnostics segment sales were “approximately $364 million,” adding that the Middle East conflict “resulting in an $11 million impact to the business in the quarter” within Diagnostics.

  • Lakkaraju reported consolidated gross margin was “52.3%,” with non-GAAP gross margin “53.1% versus 53.8%,” and attributed the year-over-year pressure to factors including “unfavorable manufacturing absorption…40 basis points,” “higher instruments versus consumables mix…30 basis points,” “higher freight fuel surcharges…20 basis points” and “FX…20 basis points.”

  • Lakkaraju said free cash flow was “$78 million,” and that Bio-Rad repurchased “176,000 shares…at a total cost of approximately $48 million,” with “approximately $237 million available for additional buybacks.”

Q&A

  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research LLC: asked why Middle East impact was “more prominent for Bio-Rad” and how it affected logistics; Jonathan DiVincenzo (President & COO) said Bio-Rad’s exposure is tied to tender wins and scale, noting the region reached “9% of the Diagnostics business,” and that Bio-Rad “expected solid high double-digit growth in that region” before the slowdown.

  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research LLC: asked about cost actions to protect earnings and reach “mid-teens operating margins”; Roop Lakkaraju (Executive VP & CFO) said Bio-Rad has “begun to tamp down discretionary spend” and is evaluating “structural things…in how we run the business,” but said it was “a little bit early” to provide specifics.

  • Brandon Couillard, Wells Fargo: asked about Q2/Q3 phasing; Lakkaraju said Bio-Rad expects “about a 5% lift from Q1 to Q2,” then “slightly into Q3,” with Q4 as “our seasonally strongest quarter,” citing quality controls batch releases and blood typing seasonality outside the Middle East.

  • Brandon Couillard, Wells Fargo: asked about ddPCR consumables softness and competitive dynamics; DiVincenzo said consumables were “soft in the quarter” across “academic and even…biopharma,” emphasized the “equation here is growing our installed base,” and added, “we are taking share from qPCR,” while Lakkaraju confirmed the consumables decline characterization: “you’re spot on in terms of low double digits.”

  • Daniel Leonard, RBC: asked whether margin offsets are being taken given “pretty severe” decrementals; Lakkaraju said Bio-Rad has “near-term actions…in process” and on broader actions said, “stay tuned,” while DiVincenzo added Bio-Rad is assessing whether there are “appropriate surcharges or ways to mitigate some of the additional costs.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative, pressing on the magnitude of the Middle East impact and decremental margins, including Daniel Leonard’s focus on “pretty severe” decrementals and Jack Meehan’s questions on how Bio-Rad will “protect and grow earnings.”

  • Management tone was slightly cautious but solution-oriented, repeatedly stressing controllables and evaluation of actions, including Lakkaraju’s “without getting into too many specifics at this time” and “stay tuned,” alongside DiVincenzo’s “we remain confident these actions will translate into improved financial performance over time.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more constructive framing around “early signs of stabilization” (Norman Schwartz, Q4 2025) and expectations that gross margin pressures were “execution-related rather than structural” (Jonathan DiVincenzo, Q4 2025), Q1 2026 carried more explicit disruption language tied to the Middle East and corresponding guidance reduction.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The most material shift was guidance: Bio-Rad moved from Q4’s 2026 currency-neutral revenue growth outlook of “between 0.5% and 1.5%” (Roop Lakkaraju, Q4 2025) to Q1’s updated “between minus 3% and plus 0.5%” (Roop Lakkaraju, Q1 2026), explicitly attributing the change to the Middle East conflict.

  • Strategic emphasis broadened from Q4’s focus on supply chain execution and QX700 launch traction to Q1’s added operational localization and tariff mitigation actions; DiVincenzo said Bio-Rad “began manufacture of select life science instruments in China for China,” to support tenders while “minimizing tariff exposure.”

  • Analysts’ questions rotated from Q4’s ddPCR growth and process chromatography recovery timeline to Q1’s concentration on geopolitical exposure, phasing, and what actions management will take to offset margin and freight/logistics pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • DiVincenzo warned the Middle East conflict “will be a significant headwind for revenue and margin for full year 2026,” and Lakkaraju said the disruption created “continued demand softness” and “challenges getting product to our channel partners and into end customers.”

  • Management cited academic funding friction as a continued Life Science constraint; DiVincenzo said there is “considerable disruption” and “a lag between funding approvals and purchasing activity,” while Lakkaraju attributed weakness to “ongoing challenges in the academic research market, particularly in the Americas.”

  • Freight and supply chain costs remained a near-term margin risk; Lakkaraju pointed to “higher freight fuel surcharges,” and in Q&A said Q2 gross margin is expected to “tick down just a tad…specific to freight.”

Final Takeaway

Bio-Rad management framed Q1 2026 execution as on-plan versus its revenue guidance but dominated by an unexpected and material Middle East disruption in Diagnostics that also pressured margins via manufacturing absorption and logistics costs. Management updated full-year 2026 guidance ranges across revenue growth, margins, and free cash flow to reflect ongoing conflict impacts, while reiterating priorities around ddPCR installed base expansion led by QX700, operational efficiency actions, and a more targeted M&A approach focused on proven businesses in the $100 million to $500 million revenue range.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rimini Street reiterates 2026 revenue growth 4%-6% while guiding Q2 revenue $106M-$108M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rimini Street (RMNI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our first quarter results reflect continued growth and accelerating momentum,” said (Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Seth Ravin), while highlighting “strong growth in adjusted calculated billings and adjusted ARR” and year-over-year expansion in remaining performance obligations “adjusted for the Oracle PeopleSoft support and services wind down.”
  • (CEO Ravin) said the company is “continuing our evolution beyond our position as the premier third-party enterprise software support provider” and added: “We are now the software support and Agentic AI ERP company.”
  • (CEO Ravin) cited large-deal activity and new logo additions: “During the quarter, we closed 11 new client transactions with over $1 million in TCV and totaling $33 million compared to 5 transactions totaling $5.6 million during the same period last year. We added 50 new logos...”
  • “Our first quarter results reflect solid execution and continued sign of momentum, highlighted by remaining performance obligations, RPO, and billings growth, along with a return to top line growth despite the headwinds from the wind-down of support and services for Oracle's PeopleSoft software,” said (Executive VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Michael Perica).

Outlook

  • (CFO Perica) said: “The company is providing second quarter 2026 revenue guidance to be in the range of $106 million to $108 million and reiterating the full year 2026 guidance provided at our Investor Day in December 2025 of revenue growth in the 4% to 6% range and adjusted EBITDA margins in the 12.5% to 15.5% range, combined to achieve Rule of 20.”
  • (CEO Ravin) explained the decision not to raise full-year targets after Q1: “We want to just take it very carefully... we want to just get another quarter under the belt and think about that before we talk about any kind of raise in the guidance.”

Financial Results

  • (CFO Perica) reported revenue of $105.5 million (up 1.2% year-over-year), and said: “Excluding support services for PeopleSoft products, revenue increased by 5.2% year-over-year.”
  • (CFO Perica) reported annualized recurring revenue of $400.8 million (up 1.2% year-over-year), revenue retention of 88%, and noted that “approximately 81% of subscription revenue [was] noncancelable for at least 12 months.”
  • (CFO Perica) reported billings of $95.3 million (up 19.9% year-over-year), and added: “When excluding billings associated with support services for PeopleSoft products, the year-over-year increase was 22.9%.”
  • (CFO Perica) reported gross margin of 59.0% (non-GAAP gross margin 59.5%) and attributed pressure to execution choices: “Our gross margin in the period was negatively impacted by investments pulled forward in the year to take advantage of market opportunities and select non-subscription engagements that had large, front-loaded start-up costs.”
  • (CFO Perica) said the company “comfortably” made “$10 million of additional voluntary principal prepayments” and ended Q1 with debt of $58.4 million and “net cash position” of $73.8 million.

Q&A

  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked for more booking context and domestic vs. international; (CEO Ravin) pointed to larger contracts and said, “60% of those deals were in North America this year,” while adding, “We're seeing continued growing pipelines.”
  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about longer-duration contracts; (CEO Ravin) said prior average length was “something short of 3 years, about 2.5, 2.6 years for a new contract,” and added: “And we're seeing longer-term contracts being signed.”
  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked for an update on Agentic AI testers and how to measure progress; (CEO Ravin) tied measurement to targets including “mid-single digit” growth and said, “We believe that we sit on surplus cash. We believe that, that should be returned to shareholders in one way or another.”
  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum: Questioned lower gross margin and higher S&M as a percent of revenue; (CEO Ravin) said expenses were pulled forward and cited investments including “our U.S. federal team... with our new GSA contract,” PE coverage, AI investments, and noted, “We're over 80 sellers now.”
  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum: Pressed why full-year guidance wasn’t raised; (CEO Ravin) said, “we're back and feeling very positive and very confident... But we want to just get another quarter under the belt.”
  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum: Asked about retention staying at 88%; (CEO Ravin) said, “We beat our internal numbers on the retention number,” and added, “Our goal is, of course, to see that TTM return to over a 90% number.”
  • Alex Fuhrman, Lucid Capital Markets: Asked about Q1 client adds and whether AI is driving demand; (CEO Ravin) said, “We absolutely are seeing improvements in everything from the number of leads coming in to lead conversion to opportunity, opportunity to closes.”
  • Alex Fuhrman, Lucid Capital Markets: Asked how large R&D could get; (CFO Perica) said, “We do expect it to creep up throughout the year and can exit the year about 1% or so.”
  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about Agentic AI revenue contribution and disclosure; (CEO Ravin) said, “it's not what we call a material amount yet,” while emphasizing the sales impact of “a path and this vision and technology.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral in profitability-focused follow-ups, particularly around margins, spending, and lack of a guidance raise, as reflected in Jeff Van Rhee’s questions on gross margin being “on the lower end” and why revenue strength did not “flow... into the annual guide.”
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and expansion-oriented; in Q&A, it was confident but cautious on guidance, with (CEO Ravin) stating, “we want to just take it very carefully,” and (CFO Perica) repeatedly tying incremental investments back to “incorporated overall in our guidance.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call placed more emphasis on spending pull-forwards (federal, PE, sales expansion, R&D line item) and margin impact, while maintaining similar confidence language around bookings momentum and longer-duration commitments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 framed the strategy as an identity shift—“We are now the software support and Agentic AI ERP company”—while Q4 centered on the launch and positioning of “next-generation Agentic AI ERP solutions” and “Rimini Agentic UX solutions powered by ServiceNow.”
  • Guidance cadence shifted from Q4’s Q1 revenue guide ($101.5 million to $103.5 million) to Q1’s Q2 revenue guide ($106 million to $108 million), with both quarters reiterating the same full-year 2026 revenue growth (4% to 6%) and adjusted EBITDA margin range (12.5% to 15.5%).
  • Q1 introduced a separate R&D line item and described it as “ongoing and increasing,” while Q4 discussion of innovation spend was embedded within broader operating expense commentary.
  • Analysts’ focus broadened from Q4’s visibility into returning to growth and AI adoption timing to Q1’s scrutiny of gross margin compression, sales and marketing intensity, and why guidance was not raised.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CFO Perica) reiterated PeopleSoft wind-down obligations: the company “will complete its previously announced wind-down... no later than July 31, 2028,” and said PeopleSoft revenue was 3% of Q1 revenue.
  • (CFO Perica) described gross margin pressure tied to “investments pulled forward” and “front-loaded start-up costs,” while (CEO Ravin) said expense ratios were expected to “normalize throughout the year.”
  • Retention remained a focal metric at 88%, with (CEO Ravin) saying the company aims for “over a 90% number” and expects improvement to “start to see it show up... starting in the next quarter or so.”

Final Takeaway

Management said Q1 marked a return to top-line growth and emphasized a shift toward longer-duration contracts, larger deal activity, and a broader positioning that combines core software support with “Agentic AI ERP” modernization. The company reiterated full-year 2026 targets (4% to 6% revenue growth and 12.5% to 15.5% adjusted EBITDA margins) and guided Q2 revenue to $106 million to $108 million, while acknowledging near-term margin pressure from pulled-forward investments in federal, private equity coverage, sales capacity, and product R&D, alongside ongoing PeopleSoft wind-down headwinds through July 31, 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: St. Joe outlines first homesite closings at Pigeon Creek in early 2027 as Q1 revenue rises 5% to $99.1M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The St. Joe Company (JOE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "For the first quarter, we had a 5% increase in revenue and an 8% increase in operating income," said (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Jorge Gonzalez), adding, "The first quarter revenue of $99.1 million was the company's highest first quarter revenue outside of the one-time timberland sale in 2014."

  • "The increase in total revenue included a 13% increase in hospitality revenue and a 4% increase in real estate revenue when compared to the same period last year," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said, while noting, "Leasing revenue decreased by 10%, which was primarily due to the sale of the Watercrest Senior Living property in September of 2025."

  • "Net income decreased by 21%, primarily because of a decrease in equity and income from unconsolidated joint ventures," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said. "Equity and income was $3.5 million for the quarter when compared to $10.2 million in the first quarter of 2025," and the company attributed the change primarily to "a lower home closing volume" at its Latitude Margaritaville Watersound JV.

  • "We continue to successfully execute our strategy of growing recurring revenue," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said, citing "a first quarter record of $44.7 million in hospitality revenue and $14.7 million in leasing revenue, which together accounted for 60% of the total revenue in the quarter."

  • "The gross margin improved across all hospitality categories to a total of 24% for the first quarter of 2026, as compared to 18% for the first quarter of 2025," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said. He also stated, "We have been focused on improving gross margins and leasing revenue, with 61% for the first quarter of 2026 when compared to 55% for the first quarter of 2025."

  • "In the first quarter, we continue to implement a measured and multifaceted capital allocation strategy with $20.7 million in capital expenditures, primarily for growth, $9.2 million in cash dividends, $5 million in share repurchases, and $10.9 million in reduction of project debt," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • "We were pleased to announce the execution of a contract with PulteGroup for up to 2,653 homesites," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said, adding, "PulteGroup is the third-largest homebuilder in the country, and this is their first entry into the Northwest Florida market." He also said the company executed "a long-range utility, water, and sewer agreement" for Lake Powell and West Laird DSAPs, where work is "planned to commence later this year."

  • "Our disclosure was intentional. We use the term significant variable revenue," said (Executive VP & CFO Marek Bakun), adding, "because we do have built-in protections" in the PulteGroup takedown structure.

Outlook

  • "Pigeon Creek, in terms of closings, it's probably going to be the early part of 2027," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said, adding, "We are actively working on the engineering and permitting of the first phase of Pigeon Creek, working very closely with PulteGroup."

  • On hospitality demand, "we are cautiously optimistic that our hospitality segment is going to have a good year and a good season this year," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • On commercial leasing and development cadence, "we are getting a lot more calls from prospective commercial tenants, particularly national tenants, than we ever have," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said; "if that trend continues, we're certainly going to make decisions to meet that demand and accelerate our commercial development."

Financial Results

  • "The first quarter revenue of $99.1 million" reflected mix changes that included "a 13% increase in hospitality revenue" and "a 4% increase in real estate revenue" year over year, (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • Profitability drivers highlighted on the call included margin expansion: "The gross margin improved across all hospitality categories to a total of 24%," and leasing gross margin was "61%" for the quarter, (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • For the Latitude JV swing, "There were no real changes in costs," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said; "The income was driven by volume, the number of closings that were delivered in the quarter compared to the first quarter of 2025," and "The actual margins on the per unit were actually above the margins a year ago quarter."

Q&A

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on Pigeon Creek DSAP takedown pace and land-value protections; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "ultimately, pace is set by the market," and Executive VP & CFO Bakun: "we do have built-in protections."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on hotel RevPAR and the New York City marketing campaign; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "The majority of the uptick was organic" and "we have seen an increase from that market in bookings so far this year," while adding, "it's still very early in the campaign."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on data center interest at Venture Crossing Enterprise Center; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We have had discussions with those types of users" and monetization could be "a ground lease" and/or "potentially a sale depending on facts and circumstances."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) requesting brokerage revenue detail; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We still don't have a full year's worth of data," and said the company plans "to open 3 additional locations" after starting in two.

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on Pier Park City Center SURF Park lease payments and other monetization; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We've made significant progress with the SURF Park" and commencement is "relatively soon," while saying the company is "being very thoughtful" about additional users.

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on why Southwood was removed from residential under-contract dollars; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "There have been no changes to the contract."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) proposing Walton County lot-sale acceleration; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "we don't want to get too far ahead of market demand" and said the company feels "very bullish" about meeting demand "at the highest prices and highest margins possible."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on becoming a more active commercial developer; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "it's going to be dependent on market demand" and "if that trend continues" of inbound tenant calls, the company could "accelerate our commercial development."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on adding lots to Latitude JV; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We've been in discussion with our partner about the next phase" and "it would be to the immediate west of the existing joint venture."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on WaterSound Club capacity; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We don't think we're at capacity."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on custom homesites near the future Arc Park; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We don't have the specifics yet" but "it is a real project that we're planning" and said to "look for us to share more information...in the subsequent weeks and months."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on migration/tourism trends; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "it still feels really positive" and said the company is seeing "more and more guests" from "a broader range of locations."

  • Submitted question (read by Executive VP & CFO Bakun) on Intercoastal Waterway Marina; President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez: "We started work on that marina" and "We still have a couple more permits" before accelerating work.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly positive analyst tone, with growth-oriented pressure on pace, pricing, and timelines (Walton County acceleration, commercial development share, Latitude lot replenishment); questions were direct on execution details such as "whether it's over 3, 5, or 10 years" and repeated requests for timing.

  • Slightly positive management tone, with measured language around demand and timing, including "we are cautiously optimistic" and "it's still very early," alongside firmer conviction on positioning such as "Our competitive advantage is clear," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • Versus Q4 2025, the Q1 2026 exchange leaned more toward near-term cadence and timing (Pigeon Creek closings, SURF Park start, marina permits) and less toward stock repurchases and balance-sheet philosophy that featured prominently in Q4 questions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 centered on pipeline conversion (PulteGroup homesites, utility agreements, timing of Pigeon Creek closings) and hospitality demand signals, while Q4 2025 emphasized full-year performance milestones, DSAP break-ground plans, and broader capital allocation commentary.

  • The recurring-revenue narrative continued, but Q1 2026 highlighted a quarterly mix point ("60% of the total revenue") and margin expansion in hospitality and leasing, whereas Q4 2025 emphasized the multi-year shift from "15% recurring revenue" historically to "56% recurring revenue" at present.

  • Analyst questioning shifted from Q4 2025’s focus on buybacks/debt level and lot pricing comps to Q1 2026’s focus on project-level execution timelines (Pigeon Creek, SURF Park, marina permits) and demand sources (NYC campaign, inbound commercial tenant calls).

Risks and Concerns

  • Earnings variability from joint ventures remained a key swing factor: "Latitude is a large-scale, long-term project that will have ebbs and flows in quarterly and even year-to-year volume," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • Timing dependencies and permitting were reiterated as gating items, including the marina where "we still have a couple more permits" and Pigeon Creek where the company is "working on the engineering and permitting," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

  • Management framed inventory pacing as a capital-allocation risk: "we also don't want to get too far ahead of market demand, where we have inventory sitting in the ground for too many years," (President, CEO, COO & Chairman of the Board Gonzalez) said.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of record first-quarter revenue (excluding the 2014 timber sale), margin expansion in hospitality and leasing, and continued emphasis on recurring revenue and measured capital allocation. Strategically, leadership pointed to the PulteGroup agreement (up to 2,653 homesites) and new utility agreements as steps to extend the development runway, while setting expectations that Pigeon Creek closings are likely in the early part of 2027 and maintaining “cautiously optimistic” language on the hospitality season and demand indicators.

data.title: Tesla revenue tied to Musk companies tops half a billion last year
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) generated over $500M in revenue last year from transactions with companies tied to CEO Elon Musk, according to an amended annual filing.

The bulk of the revenue, about $430.1M, came from dealings with xAI (X.AI), which Tesla had initially disclosed in January. In the updated filing released Thursday, the company added another $143.3M in revenue from SpaceX (SPACE), primarily for the sale of vehicles.

Tesla didn’t provide the latter figure in its initial annual filing released in January.

The cross-company activity has continued into 2026. Tesla said it recognized roughly $78.1M in revenue from xAI through February 2026, primarily for the sale of its Megapack products, as well as approximately $0.1M in revenue from SpaceX.

The disclosures come as links between Musk’s businesses deepen. xAI has been purchasing Tesla’s Megapack energy storage systems, while Tesla has begun integrating xAI’s Grok chatbot into its vehicles. Musk has also outlined plans for Tesla and SpaceX to collaborate on chip manufacturing.

SpaceX acquired xAI in February, bringing rockets, satellites, AI and plans for space-based data centers under one roof.

data.title: Corcept raises 2026 revenue guidance to $950M-$1.05B as it targets $2B Cushing’s sales and $1B+ Lifyorli by decade-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $164.9 million... We have increased our 2026 revenue guidance to $950 million to $1.05 billion. Net loss was $31.8 million... Our cash and investments at March 31 were $515 million.” (CFO & Treasurer Atabak Mokari)
  • Sean Maduck said demand rose with “a record number of new prescriptions written from a record number of prescribers,” and highlighted a near-term revenue timing lag from “onerous reauthorization procedures,” early-stage patient ramp, and “the insurance prior authorization backlog” tied to the specialty pharmacy transition.
  • Roberto Vieira framed the quarter around the FDA approval and early launch indicators for Lifyorli, emphasizing ROSELLA’s survival results and launch execution: “36 days into our launch, things are going very well... Prescriptions have already been written by over 200 physicians... Lifyorli's inclusion in the National Comprehensive Cancer Network... guidelines as a preferred regimen just 15 days after approval will support strong adoption and payer access.” (President of Oncology Vieira)
  • “In April, we met with the FDA regarding relacorilant's new drug application... We will provide an update on relacorilant's regulatory path in the near future.” (Co-Founder, President, CEO & Director Joseph K. Belanoff)

Outlook

  • Mokari attributed the higher full-year range to the combined read-through from both franchises: “our endocrine business represents the bulk of our guidance range just given where we are with oncology,” while citing oncology updates including “approval,” “final ROSELLA results in The Lancet,” and “inclusion in the NCCN guidelines,” and concluding, “we are confidently raising our guidance range.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Management reiterated long-term revenue ambitions on both pillars: “We expect our current Cushing's Syndrome business to grow to at least $2 billion in annual revenue by the end of this decade.” (President of Endocrinology Sean Maduck) and “We expect Lifyorli only to exceed $1 billion in annual revenue in the United States by the end of the decade.” (President of Oncology Vieira)

Financial Results

  • “Our revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $164.9 million compared to $157.2 million in the prior year period.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • “Net loss was $31.8 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to net income of $20.5 million in the first quarter of last year.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Mokari tied the raised 2026 revenue outlook to operating momentum across divisions: “given the strength on both sides,” management said it expects the endocrinology fundamentals and the new oncology approval dynamics to translate into revenue.

Q&A

  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: With updated guidance, is it mostly relacorilant and have Korlym assumptions changed?; CFO Mokari: “our endocrine business represents the bulk of our guidance range... only a small portion... comes from our oncology business,” adding the raise reflects oncology milestones and strong Cushing’s fundamentals.
  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: Positioning vs. KEYTRUDA?; President of Oncology Vieira: “we only compete in a subset of the market,” citing KEYTRUDA’s PD-L1 use and saying physicians show “a preference... to actually look into the ROSELLA regimen... as being a preferred regimen.”
  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: How does nenocorilant differ from relacorilant?; Chief Development Officer William Guyer: “nenocorilant has shown strong activity in animal models in combination with immunotherapy,” and the Phase I study is intended to guide “rapidly move forward into a Phase II study.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: What share could Lifyorli take with NCCN preferred status?; President of Oncology Vieira: Corcept is “targeting 5,000 physicians” and said it has “every expectation” of adoption, adding, “we do have an expectation of becoming market leader in a relatively short time frame.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Phase III DAZALS design/dose/endpoints/timing?; CDO Guyer: “the endpoint would be survival and the focus would be 300 milligrams,” and the team expects to “start this trial this year and enroll patients by the end of this year.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Add another specialty pharmacy?; President of Endocrinology Maduck: “our plan is down the road to expand our network,” and “our plan is to bring in some additional support in the fourth quarter this year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was probing and execution-focused, with repeated questions on guidance drivers, competitive positioning (KEYTRUDA), launch share, and operational capacity (specialty pharmacy).
  • Management tone was confident and promotional around approvals and studies, using phrases such as “we are confident” and “things are going very well,” while providing specific near-term operational actions (e.g., adding pharmacy network support in Q4).
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 discussion was less centered on regulatory surprise and more centered on post-approval launch execution and raised revenue guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest shift from Q4 to Q1 was the transition from anticipating an oncology PDUFA decision to reporting an FDA approval: “The FDA's approval of Lifyorli... 3.5 months ahead of its PDUFA date.” (President of Oncology Vieira)
  • Revenue guidance moved higher from Q4’s “$900 million to $1 billion” to Q1’s “$950 million to $1.05 billion.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Q4 emphasized relacorilant’s Cushing’s complete response letter and next steps; Q1 emphasized an FDA meeting on relacorilant’s NDA and said, “We will provide an update on relacorilant's regulatory path in the near future.” (CEO Belanoff)
  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s CRL and Korlym pricing dynamics toward Q1’s launch uptake, competitive framing, and pipeline differentiation (nenocorilant) alongside operational scaling.

Risks and Concerns

  • Maduck cited revenue headwinds from payer processes and execution friction: “insurance companies impose onerous reauthorization procedures... that interrupt patient coverage,” and the new pharmacy must work through “the insurance prior authorization backlog.”
  • Belanoff highlighted the need for regulatory clarity on relacorilant in Cushing’s syndrome, stating the company will update investors after FDA interactions.
  • For ALS development, Belanoff said DAZALS discontinuations were driven by tolerability and mitigation is in progress: “Nonserious GI distress caused most of the discontinuations... we think can be avoided,” with a dose-titration study informing the planned pivotal trial.

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as a quarter of raised full-year revenue guidance, accelerating endocrinology demand signals that are expected to flow through as payer and pharmacy friction clears, and a faster-than-expected oncology milestone with Lifyorli approved ahead of the PDUFA date and showing early breadth of prescribing. Leadership said it will update the market “in the near future” on relacorilant’s Cushing’s regulatory path, while advancing multiple catalysts with timelines including BELLA’s first-arm results by year-end, MONARCH (MASH) data by year-end, and a Phase III ALS study start and enrollment expected later this year.
